{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
  ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Zero Trust
  A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
  It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the network perimeter,
  and therefore no user, device, or system should be trusted by default — even
  if they are already inside the network.

Defense in Depth
  A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
  controls to protect systems and data. The idea is that if one layer fails,
  the next layer will catch the threat. It is often described as a "castle
  and moat" approach with concentric rings of protection.

================================================================================

2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  a) Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize every access request based
     on all available data points (user identity, device health, location,
     service, data classification, etc.).
  b) Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum level of access necessary
     for a user or system to perform its function. Use Just-In-Time (JIT) and
     Just-Enough-Access (JEA) models.
  c) Assume Breach — Operate as if the network has already been compromised.
     Segment networks, encrypt data end-to-end, and continuously monitor for
     anomalies.
  d) Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones so that
     lateral movement by attackers is severely limited.
  e) Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Continuously inspect and log all
     traffic, re-verify credentials, and apply dynamic risk scoring.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  a) Layered Controls — Deploy security at multiple levels: physical,
     perimeter, network, host, application, and data.
  b) Redundancy — No single control is relied upon; overlapping controls
     compensate for the failure of any one layer.
  c) Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
     detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors to avoid
     single points of failure.
  d) Perimeter-First Thinking — Traditionally begins with a strong external
     boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) and works inward.
  e) Depth Across Domains — Applies to people, processes, and technology
     across the entire IT stack.

================================================================================

3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                       | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                   |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust is a vulnerability;        | Layers reduce risk; perimeter      |
|                             | trust nothing by default.        | is the first line of defense.      |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Network Assumption          | The network is always hostile;   | The internal network is safer      |
|                             | no distinction between internal  | than the external; there is a      |
|                             | and external traffic.            | trusted inside zone.               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Identity-centric; every access   | Perimeter-centric; once inside,    |
|                             | request is evaluated per session.| access is relatively open.         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Protecting data and resources    | Protecting the network and         |
|                             | regardless of location.          | infrastructure layers.             |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Actively prevented via micro-    | Slowed but not always prevented;   |
|                             | segmentation and per-session     | relies on internal layers to       |
|                             | authentication.                  | detect and contain.                |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Architecture                | Modern, cloud-native, designed   | Traditional, on-premises roots,    |
|                             | for distributed workforces.      | can be adapted to cloud.           |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Implementation Complexity   | High — requires re-architecting  | Moderate — builds on existing      |
|                             | identity, network, and apps.     | infrastructure incrementally.      |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+

================================================================================

4. HOW THEY COMPLEMENT E

{"content": "================================================================================
  ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Zero Trust
  A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
  It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the network perimeter,
  and therefore no user, device, or system should be trusted by default — even
  if they are already inside the network.

Defense in Depth
  A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
  controls to protect systems and data. The idea is that if one layer fails,
  the next layer will catch the threat. It is often described as a "castle
  and moat" approach with concentric rings of protection.

================================================================================

2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  a) Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize every access request based
     on all available data points (user identity, device health, location,
     service, data classification, etc.).
  b) Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum level of access necessary
     for a user or system to perform its function. Use Just-In-Time (JIT) and
     Just-Enough-Access (JEA) models.
  c) Assume Breach — Operate as if the network has already been compromised.
     Segment networks, encrypt data end-to-end, and continuously monitor for
     anomalies.
  d) Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones so that
     lateral movement by attackers is severely limited.
  e) Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Continuously inspect and log all
     traffic, re-verify credentials, and apply dynamic risk scoring.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  a) Layered Controls — Deploy security at multiple levels: physical,
     perimeter, network, host, application, and data.
  b) Redundancy — No single control is relied upon; overlapping controls
     compensate for the failure of any one layer.
  c) Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
     detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors to avoid
     single points of failure.
  d) Perimeter-First Thinking — Traditionally begins with a strong external
     boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) and works inward.
  e) Depth Across Domains — Applies to people, processes, and technology
     across the entire IT stack.

================================================================================

3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                       | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                   |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust is a vulnerability;        | Layers reduce risk; perimeter      |
|                             | trust nothing by default.        | is the first line of defense.      |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Network Assumption          | The network is always hostile;   | The internal network is safer      |
|                             | no distinction between internal  | than the external; there is a      |
|                             | and external traffic.            | trusted inside zone.               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Identity-centric; every access   | Perimeter-centric; once inside,    |
|                             | request is evaluated per session.| access is relatively open.         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Protecting data and resources    | Protecting the network and         |
|                             | regardless of location.          | infrastructure layers.             |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Actively prevented via micro-    | Slowed but not always prevented;   |
|                             | segmentation and per-session     | relies on internal layers to       |
|                             | authentication.                  | detect and contain.                |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Architecture                | Modern, cloud-native, designed   | Traditional, on-premises roots,    |
|                             | for distributed workforces.      | can be adapted to cloud.           |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Implementation Complexity   | High — requires re-architecting  | Moderate — builds on existing      |
|                             | identity, network, and apps.     | infrastructure incrementally.      |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+

================================================================================

4. HOW THEY COMPLEMENT EACH OTHER
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Zero Trust and Defense in Depth are not mutually exclusive. In fact, they
work best together:

  • Defense in Depth provides the layered framework within which Zero Trust
    principles can be applied. For example, micro-segmentation (a

     Segment networks, encrypt data end-to-end, and continuously monitor for
     anomalies.
  d) Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones so that
     lateral movement by attackers is severely limited.
  e) Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Continuously inspect and log all
     traffic, re-verify credentials, and apply dynamic risk scoring.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  a) Layered Controls — Deploy security at multiple levels: physical,
     perimeter, network, host, application, and data.
  b) Redundancy — No single control is relied upon; overlapping controls
     compensate for the failure of any one layer.
  c) Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
     detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors to avoid
     single points of failure.
  d) Perimeter-First Thinking — Traditionally begins with a strong external
     boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) and works inward.
  e) Depth Across Domains — Applies to people, processes, and technology
     across the entire IT stack.

================================================================================

3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                       | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                   |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust is a vulnerability;        | Layers reduce risk; perimeter      |
|                             | trust nothing by default.        | is the first line of defense.      |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Network Assumption          | The network is always hostile;   | The internal network is safer      |
|                             | no distinction between internal  | than the external; there is a      |
|                             | and external traffic.            | trusted inside zone.               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Identity-centric; every access   | Perimeter-centric; once inside,    |
|                             | request is evaluated per session.| access is relatively open.         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Protecting data and resources    | Protecting the network and         |
|                             | regardless of location.          | infrastructure layers.             |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Actively prevented via micro-    | Slowed but not always prevented;   |
|                             | segmentation and per-session     | relies on internal layers to       |
|                             | authentication.                  | detect and contain.                |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Architecture                | Modern, cloud-native, designed   | Traditional, on-premises roots,    |
|                             | for distributed workforces.      | can be adapted to cloud.           |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Implementation Complexity   | High — requires re-architecting  | Moderate — builds on existing      |
|                             | identity, network, and apps.     | infrastructure incrementally.      |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+

================================================================================

4. HOW THEY COMPLEMENT EACH OTHER
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Zero Trust and Defense in Depth are not mutually exclusive. In fact

{"path": "workspace/security_comparison.txt", "content": "================================================================================
  ZERO TRUST vs. DEFENSE IN DEPTH: A COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS
================================================================================

1. OVERVIEW
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Zero Trust
  A security model centered on the principle of "never trust, always verify."
  It assumes that threats exist both outside and inside the network perimeter,
  and therefore no user, device, or system should be trusted by default — even
  if they are already inside the network.

Defense in Depth
  A layered security strategy that employs multiple, overlapping defensive
  controls to protect systems and data. The idea is that if one layer fails,
  the next layer will catch the threat. It is often described as a "castle
  and moat" approach with concentric rings of protection.

================================================================================

2. CORE PRINCIPLES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

ZERO TRUST PRINCIPLES:
  a) Verify Explicitly — Authenticate and authorize every access request based
     on all available data points (user identity, device health, location,
     service, data classification, etc.).
  b) Least Privilege Access — Grant only the minimum level of access necessary
     for a user or system to perform its function. Use Just-In-Time (JIT) and
     Just-Enough-Access (JEA) models.
  c) Assume Breach — Operate as if the network has already been compromised.
     Segment networks, encrypt data end-to-end, and continuously monitor for
     anomalies.
  d) Micro-Segmentation — Break the network into small, isolated zones so that
     lateral movement by attackers is severely limited.
  e) Continuous Monitoring & Validation — Continuously inspect and log all
     traffic, re-verify credentials, and apply dynamic risk scoring.

DEFENSE IN DEPTH PRINCIPLES:
  a) Layered Controls — Deploy security at multiple levels: physical,
     perimeter, network, host, application, and data.
  b) Redundancy — No single control is relied upon; overlapping controls
     compensate for the failure of any one layer.
  c) Diversity of Defenses — Use different types of controls (preventive,
     detective, corrective, deterrent) from different vendors to avoid
     single points of failure.
  d) Perimeter-First Thinking — Traditionally begins with a strong external
     boundary (firewalls, IDS/IPS) and works inward.
  e) Depth Across Domains — Applies to people, processes, and technology
     across the entire IT stack.

================================================================================

3. KEY DIFFERENCES
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| ASPECT                      | ZERO TRUST                       | DEFENSE IN DEPTH                   |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Philosophy                  | Trust is a vulnerability;        | Layers reduce risk; perimeter      |
|                             | trust nothing by default.        | is the first line of defense.      |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Network Assumption          | The network is always hostile;   | The internal network is safer      |
|                             | no distinction between internal  | than the external; there is a      |
|                             | and external traffic.            | trusted inside zone.               |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Access Control              | Identity-centric; every access   | Perimeter-centric; once inside,    |
|                             | request is evaluated per session.| access is relatively open.         |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Primary Focus               | Protecting data and resources    | Protecting the network and         |
|                             | regardless of location.          | infrastructure layers.             |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Lateral Movement            | Actively prevented via micro-    | Slowed but not always prevented;   |
|                             | segmentation and per-session     | relies on internal layers to       |
|                             | authentication.                  | detect and contain.                |
+-----------------------------+----------------------------------+------------------------------------+

|                             | for distributed workforces.      | can be adapted to cloud.           |
